data.title: PTY: Attractive Valuation And Dividend Is Still Supported
data.description:
Overview

As the debt market continues to face headwinds, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Opportunity Fund (PTY) has continued to decline in value. At the time of my prior coverage, I issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation and attractive outlook at the time. Since then, the fund has continued to decline in value, and the interest rate environment has shifted. Additionally, the fund has released an updated semi-annual report that provides transparency into the earnings. Despite the headwinds of the sector, I believe that PTY remains a great opportunity for investors that want to secure a stream of supplemental dividend income.

When I previously covered PTY, the fund traded at a premium to NAV of 10.75%. Following the slight pullback in its share price, PTY now trades at a smaller premium to NAV of 4.83%. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that PTY continues to trade at one of the most attractive price-to-NAV levels over the last ten-year period. Even on a smaller time frame, PTY has traded at an average premium to NAV of 21.7%. Therefore, this serves as an excellent time to accumulate shares if you are a long-term income investor looking for a good opportunity in the market.

PTY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 11.7% while issuing those payouts on a monthly basis. Despite the attractive valuation of the fund, I do believe that additional downside risks remain. Unfortunately, growth for the fund may be limited as long as interest rates remain elevated. The reality is that a debt-focused fund like PTY is almost entirely reliant on a healthy debt market, and since interest rates are still near the highest end of its ten-year range, the risk of defaults continues to be relevant. The fund's thinner operating margins will also limit the rate of NAV growth in the near term, which can threaten dividend sustainability.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fund overview, PTY has total managed assets of $3.3B that are spread across a diverse range of income-producing securities. The fund's primary goal is to generate a high current income through various types of income securities, so investors should understand that PTY's performance will not be correlated with the movement of the indices since the portfolio does not hold equities. Furthermore, the fund provides some global exposure, and this can be great for investors seeking a bit of diversity away from the U.S. markets. However, the majority of the fund is still allocated to U.S. positions, with the full portfolio breakdown as follows:

  • United States: 59.39%
  • France: 5.10%
  • Bermuda: 3.39%
  • Germany: 3.22%
  • Colombia: 2.90%

PTY is mostly allocated to high-yield credit securities, so the fund's success is ultimately reliant on borrowers' ability to keep up with their required debt maintenance. This is followed by an allocation to other income-producing assets like Non-USD developed and emerging market securities, which account for 16.03% and 15.52% of assets, respectively. Expanding on this, the fund maintains an efficient allocation across different sectors.

Most notably, the fund is most heavily allocated to technology investments at 11.89% of assets. During a period where markets are displaying pessimism towards SaaS companies, this could increase the overall risk profile of the fund. Software companies are seeing their moats threatened by the capabilities and cost efficiency of LLMs like Gemini, Claude, and ChatGPT. So while it isn't specifically highlighted anywhere in fund documents, this exposure to technology positions could be negatively impacting earnings. On the bright side, the portfolio is very diversified across many other areas of the market to help reduce any sort of concentration risks.

Although most of the fund's portfolio is allocated to securities with short-dated maturities, the fund continues to display a sensitivity to the high-interest rate environment. Shorter-dated securities are typically more resilient to high-interest rates, but this has not been the case for PTY, likely due to the fund's use of leverage. For reference, the funds breakdown by maturity is as follows:

  • 0-1 Years: 22.56%
  • 1-3 Years: 19.98%
  • 3-5 Years: 24.78%
  • 5-10 Years: 19.78%
  • 10-20 Years: 8.15%
  • 20+ Years: 4.74%

The fund now has total effective leverage of 17.55% of assets, which is equivalent to ~$521.5M. Although moderate, this can still increase the overall risk profile for the fund if allocated to positions that decline in value. As we can see below, the fund has struggled to regain any sort of positive momentum since interest rates were increased throughout 2022 and 2023. Since the fund is using leverage, the operating spread between the cost of its leverage and the yield collected from securities will be thinner, making it more difficult for leverage to be used efficiently. Therefore, high-interest rates are likely to continue suppressing the share price in the near term.

Performance Insights & Outlook

Since the time of my last coverage, PTY has released an updated semi-annual report for the first half of its 2026 period. Net investment income amounted to $0.59 per share, while net realized gains landed at $0.17 per share. Assuming PTY is capable of achieving a similar level of net investment income over the remainder of the current period, the net investment income level will fall at the lower end of its historical range. Net investment income consists of the interest income collected from its securities, and the decline may be attributed to the negative impact of leverage.

Furthermore, net investment income doesn't support distributions on its own, so this leaves the fund quite reliant on net realized gains to bridge that gap in its earnings. Under normal market conditions this would be a fine dynamic, but the high-interest rate environment means that the fund is very unlikely to see its positions rise, which means net realized gains will be limited. This becomes an issue since it means that PTY may find itself in a position where it is paying out more than it earns, which will directly cause the NAV to erode. This is one of the reasons why the NAV has been unable to reach the highs of 2021. For reference, here are the year-end NAV amounts:

  • 2020: $12.44 per share
  • 2021: $14.40 per share
  • 2022: $11.21 per share
  • 2023: $10.83 per share
  • 2024: $11.17 per share
  • 2025: $11.73 per share
  • 2026 (semi-annual): $11.92 per share

So when an investor holds PTY for a long time horizon, this dynamic translates to very little upside price growth unless interest rates are low. For instance, the fund's share price is now down by 10.46% over the last ten years. While the fund did experience some upside growth in the past, these gains were eliminated as soon as interest rates were hiked to their ten-year highs. When including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to 143.1% over the same time frame.

Looking forward, I anticipate that performance will substantially improve once interest rates begin to trend lower. The only challenge is that it can be hard to estimate how low interest rates will fall since the macro environment can change so rapidly. Even during the latest Fed meeting, it was decided that interest rates would remain unchanged. There are two ways that lower interest rates will improve performance:

  1. Improve the fund's operating spread and margins for existing positions. A lower cost of debt will provide relief and reveal some new investment opportunities.
  2. The market will see less risk associated with the chance of defaults from debt-focused securities.

The issue is that the prolonged period of elevated interest rates has probably lasted longer than the market originally anticipated. This higher cost of debt has put pressure on borrowers and makes it challenging to keep up with required debt maintenance. Since PTY has an allocation to securities that are below investment grade, this increases the fund's exposure to being impacted by defaults. For reference, the fund's breakdown by credit rating is as follows:

  • Investment Grade (AAA - BBB): 58.89%
  • Below Investment Grade (BB - D): 34.14%
  • Not Rated: 6.98%

Dividend Risk

As of the latest declared monthly distribution of $0.1188 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 11.7%. Assuming that the fund decides to keep payouts unchanged for the next twelve months, we can assume that PTY will pay out an annual amount of $1.4256 per share. Referring back to the same semi-annual report for the period, PTY is on track to support the payouts with earnings. PTY reported total earnings of $0.76 per share for the semi-annual period while issuing distributions totaling $0.71 per share. So despite the variable earnings over the last few years, the fund has still managed to support its distributions.

Looking back over the payout history over the last ten years, we can see that management decided to reduce payouts in mid-2021. This could have been done in anticipation of the fluctuation in interest rates, but I think it was a good thing. Management's decision to cut distributions shows that they are proactive when assessing the macro environment, which can help preserve the NAV through periods of uncertainty. So the decision to keep distributions consistent right now can be an indication that management is confident in their ability to continue generating sufficient earnings.

Although the fund is currently supporting distributions, the coverage is still thin. In a scenario where interest rates do not trend lower or the portfolio experiences some deterioration, the total earnings may decline and threaten the sustainability of payouts. Even if earnings continue to support distributions, a scenario where they don't grow will continue to threaten the rate of the NAV growth.

Despite this, management's ability to keep payouts within a consistent range is still a notable accomplishment. This is because it allows income investors to create their own dividend growth. I ran a backtest of an original $100,000 investment, which assumes that all dividends were reinvested, but no additional capital was ever invested after that initial $100k. By simply remaining invested and reinvesting distributions, an investor would have seen their annual income nearly double over the last few years.

2017 Dividend Income: $11,410

2025 Dividend Income: $22,673

Lastly, investors need to consider that an investment in PTY may not offer tax efficiency. For instance, the latest section 19(a) notice indicates that 87.88% of the latest distribution was supported by net investment income, which has the highest tax burden for investors. Therefore, the fund may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to limit their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that the fund remains an attractive buy at this time due to the price-to-NAV valuation and the ability to support distributions. Despite the fund's NAV not growing, the earnings still outpace the distributions being paid, which should keep the share price stable. Once interest rates start to trend lower, this should boost the fund's share price and earnings potential. The challenge is that we don't know how much longer it will take for interest rates to fall back towards their pre-pandemic levels. The fund maintains a healthy allocation towards higher-rated securities, which should help PTY remain a bit more resilient during a scenario where the debt markets continue to face headwinds. For this reason, PTY remains an attractive buy, and the ability to support distributions means that it can still serve as a powerful income compounder for long-term investors.

data.title: Ormat Technologies: The AI Investment Not On People's Radar
data.description:
Investing in the "Stranded" Solution: The Bull Case for Geothermal

Investing often requires finding solutions in unexpected places—specifically where others aren't looking. While the "herd" follows well-worn paths, a contrarian mindset identifies where the world is advancing before the shift becomes obvious. Geothermal energy represents one of these opportunities, in my opinion.

The Geothermal Landscape

Naturally, public investing options for geothermal energy are currently limited. The primary player is Ormat Technologies (ORA), the world’s only vertically integrated geothermal company. Ormat handles everything: exploration, drilling, manufacturing, and operation. Despite its dominant position, its market capitalization sits under $7 billion, illustrating how early it is, to invest in geothermal energy.

Ormat is the world’s only vertically integrated geothermal company that explores, drills, designs, builds, manufactures, owns, finances and operates geothermal power plants.

Other companies are beginning to pivot toward this space:

  • Constellation Energy (CEG): Owns "The Geysers" in California, the largest geothermal project in the U.S. (acquired via Calpine in early 2025).

  • Helmerich & Payne (HP) & Liberty (LBRT): Traditional oilfield services companies now applying their drilling expertise to geothermal heat.

  • Fervo Energy: A private leader in "enhanced" geothermal, backed by early-stage investments from players like Liberty and Devon Energy (DVN)

The Macro Problems

1. Energy Demand

The EIA projects global energy demand will grow by 50% by 2050, driven largely by Asian economies. More urgently, the IEA expects demand from AI, data centers, and cryptocurrency to double between 2022 and 2026. While the MSCI Sustainability Institute notes this is a small slice of the global 172,000 TWh used annually, the rate of growth is already straining existing grids.

2. Clean Energy Shift

Regardless of personal skepticism regarding climate change, the "prevailing winds" of global capital are moving toward decarbonization. Financial centers such as New York City are committed to monetizing carbon credits; this movement won't necessarily kill hydrocarbon energy, but it creates a sizable vacuum that must somehow be filled by "green" baseload power.

3. Geographic Constraint

The primary weakness of geothermal energy has always been its "stranded" nature. Unlike coal or gas, you cannot ship geothermal heat in a boat or train, and you can't control where the geothermal energy will be located. You must use the power exactly where the resource sits, and those resources are often in remote, unpopulated regions.

The Solutions: Why AI and Crypto Change the Game

Historically, a utility company needed to generate power near its customers. Today, technology has decoupled the location of power from the location of the user.

Solving the "Location" Problem

AI and proof-of-work mining (like Bitcoin) are the "perfect customers" for geothermal. They do not need to be located in a city; they only need a data connection.

  • Transporting Information vs. Electricity: It is expensive and inefficient to transport electricity over thousands of miles of wires. However, it is incredibly cheap to transport information via fiber optics or satellite.

  • The New Paradigm: Companies can now build massive compute centers directly on top of remote geothermal sources. The "compute" happens at the energy source, and the resulting data (the AI answer or the mined Bitcoin) is sent wirelessly to the end user.

Geothermal's Unique Edge

Unlike solar or wind, geothermal provides baseload power. It runs 24/7, regardless of whether the sun is shining or the wind is blowing. For AI data centers that require constant, steady uptime, geothermal is the only "green" solution that matches the reliability of hydrocarbons or nuclear without the threat posed by nuclear. And this is exactly what the management highlighted in their most recent earnings call in February. They are seeing growing demand from hyperscalers such as Google (GOOGL)(GOOG), Microsoft (MSFT), Amazon (AMZN), etc., who need the constant baseload energy that wind and solar cannot provide.

Free Cash Flow

2020 2021 2022 2023 2024 2025
CF from Ops ($MM) 265.0 258.8 281.0 309.4 410.9 335.1

% Growth

 NA -2.6% 8.6% 10.1% 32.8% 18.5%
Capex ($MM) (320.8) (419.3) (563.5) (618.4) (487.7) (619.8)
FCF ($MM) (55.8) (160.5) (282.5) (309) (76.8) (284.7)

Based on the 2025 10-K, cash flow from operations in 2025 was hit by a deferred income tax that came due as well as several other one-time items. This should not be interpreted as a trend.

In their most recent presentation, the company projected a capital expenditure spend in 2026 of $675 million. The company continues to outspend the cash flow, which to some will be viewed negatively. To others who believe the future is geothermal, this is just a part of investing in a growth-oriented company.

Ormat Technologies Q4 & FY2025 Results

Ormat Technologies reported its annual and Q4 results at the end of February. They delivered strong performance in 2025, with total revenues climbing 12.5% to $989.6 million. This was fueled by growth in its product and energy storage segments. The company showed significant fourth-quarter momentum, reporting $276 million in revenue. This was 19.6% above 2024 and was above analyst expectations.

The core electricity segment remains the company's financial anchor, contributing $693.9 million. However, the energy storage division was high-growth, with revenues increasing 109% to $79 million following the successful commissioning of major projects like Bottleneck and Lower Valley. This growth in the energy storage division looks poised to continue.

Supported by a 1.6 GW global generating portfolio and strategic partnerships with tech giants like Google, Ormat issued an optimistic 2026 outlook, projecting revenues to cross the $1.1 billion milestone for the first time. This trajectory, backed by a 1 GW development pipeline, reinforces management's commitment to reaching up to 2.8 GW of total capacity by 2028. The size of the pipeline relative to their current capacity demonstrates their growth potential. This shows Ormat Technologies is beginning to capitalize on the critical baseload power needs of AI and data centers.

This slide from their recent quarterly presentation highlights that the demand is coming.

Ormat Technologies Metric Comparison to Utilities Sector

Right now, Ormat is rated as a Sell according to Seeking Alpha's quant system, with an overall grade of D+ for its growth metrics and a rating of F based on valuation. But is this rating earned?

Growth

When you compare Ormat Technologies to the Utilities sector, what we find is that Ormat is outperforming in key areas. Based on my financial opinion, Ormat is underperforming in less critical areas for a company that is geared towards growth. I've split the categories out below to show the ones that Ormat is outperforming in.

Growth Metrics

Metric ORA (Value) Sector Median
Revenue Growth (YoY) 12.49% 6.89%
Revenue Growth (FWD) 9.74% 6.28%
EPS FWD Long-Term Growth (3-5Y CAGR) 10.00% 7.71%
ROE Growth (FWD) 2.29% 1.74%
CAPEX Growth (YoY) 27.09% 12.34%

Ormat Technologies underperforms in areas that are more measurements of the bottom line. For a company that is investing in growth like Ormat, one might expect their bottom line to suffer from higher capital expenditures while their top line grows disproportionately. The question becomes, is Ormat Technologies investing in profitable growth.

Valuation

Ormat Technologies receives an F grade on valuation from Seeking Alpha's quantitative system. Ormat is currently being valued as a growth stock relative to its utility energy peers. And this is likely rightfully so. But is it too overvalued? Here are the valuation metrics where you can see for yourself.

Iran and Geopolitics

A fairly recent development is the geopolitics in the Middle East and the loss of dependable energy flows through the Strait of Hormuz. This development has widespread implications that the world won't have sorted out for a long time, I suspect. Nor do I think this conflict has been resolved, just temporarily postponed. Although the United States has a strong supply of hydrocarbons, both US data centers and other areas of the world will no doubt begin thinking longer term about how they can reach geographical energy independence as well as how to become more immune to hydrocarbon price volatility.

Conclusion: Skating to Where the Puck is Headed

Headquartered in Reno, Nevada, Ormat Technologies sits at the intersection of profitability and the "green" transition. While the U.S. has ample hydrocarbons and a renewed interest in nuclear, geothermal offers a unique opportunity for patient investors who can see where the "puck" is headed.

The puck is not at the remote geothermal well yet, and it's really not even close to it, but as AI demand continues to explode and the requirement for carbon-neutral baseload power intensifies, my suspicion is the world will have no choice.

Based on valuation and challenging growth, I rate Ormat Technologies a Hold. There are likely better investments levered to artificial intelligence in the short-term. But if you like to be diversified and have a long-term view, Ormat Technologies is a lesser known way to invest in green energy that is and will increasingly be leveraged to artificial intelligence.

data.title: Targa Resources: Growth Supports A Dividend Hike With More To Come
data.description:
Shares of Targa Resources (TRGP) have been a strong performer over the past year, gaining about 35%. After languishing for much of 2025, shares have gained momentum in 2026 thanks to its differentiated growth profile and solid results. I last covered shares in November, when I rated the stock a “buy” given its strong underlying cash flow and growth prospects. I argued that shares were left behind because its lower dividend profile left it overlooked by traditional midstream investors, but that its growth would provide upside. That call has played out well, with shares gaining 38%, significantly outpacing the market’s 9% run. After such a run and a dividend increase, now is a good time to revisit TRGP.

Targa Boosts Its Dividend 25%

Now in the midstream sector, investors typically focus on income. Most MLPs and related companies pay substantial dividends, which has made them attractive to income-oriented investors. A pushback I have sometimes received in my bullish analysis of Targa is that it pays a comparatively low dividend. I have argued that this should not dissuade buyers, as it is in the midst of a large cap-ex program, using some of its cash flow. At the same time, it has been more aggressive using buybacks, given they are more flexible, while it would likely steadily raise its dividend over multiple years as projects come online. To that point, Targa repurchased $642 million of stock in 2025, and these buybacks reduced its share count by about 2%.

In fact, I argued in November that the company would likely raise its dividend by 20% annually through 2028. Sure enough, on Friday, Targe Resources increased its dividend by 25% to $1.25, giving shares a prospective dividend yield of 2.1%. This yield is still well below peers (often in the 6-8% range), but the pace of growth is powerful and will help to steadily narrow the gap. After facing pressure during COVID, it has since increased its dividend from $0.10 to $1.25 since the end of 2021. Over the next two years, I see its dividend rising to $1.80-$2.00. While Targa may not offer as much current income, it can generate substantial income growth, which may create a better total return package for investors.

Now, while the pandemic is (hopefully) a once-in-a-lifetime shock, it is entirely reasonable for investors to look at the dividend history and fear that while TRGP has been growing its payout during this economic expansion, the payout could be cut again if there is a downturn. In that case, current dividend growth would not be sustainable and would undermine any bullish thesis. Importantly, TRGP has built a more sustainable, diverse business via acquisitions and organic projects.

Targa Has A Resilient Model

90% of its cash flow is now fee-based, and even on the 10% that is commodity-exposed, it has hedged the majority of that exposure to lock in margins. Because of this, a 30% move in commodity prices only impacts EBITDA by about 2%. This shift towards fee-based contracting has significantly improved the stability of its cash flows, making its dividend more secure. Of course, over the longer term, if prices stay very low, upstream producers may drill and produce less, reducing long-term volumes and growth. That risk is not unique to Targa, of course.

Importantly, Targa has a solid balance sheet, which enables this cap-ex spending. It ended 2025 with leverage at 3.5x given its $17.4 billion of debt, right at the midpoint of its 3-4x target. As you can see below, it has invested billions in growing out its system while also reducing leverage. Another reason it was forced to slash its dividend in 2020 was that it had an over-leveraged balance sheet with debt north of 5x, which made it more vulnerable to a downturn.

For greater perspective, the difference between 5x and 3.5x leverage at Targa’s current size is about ~$7.5 billion of debt. If Targa was carrying ~$25 billion in debt today, I would be more concerned about its balance sheet and dividend sustainability. It now has significant financial headroom that provides more flexibility and stability for shareholders. The fact it has been able to invest over $10 billion in cap-ex and M&A while cutting leverage speaks to the benefit of its fee-based model and advantaged Permian footprint.

Targa Has A Leading Growth Profile

These benefits are showing up in its financial results, too. In the company’s fourth quarter, Targa earned $545 million even as revenue declined by 8% to $4.1 billion. Adjusted EBITDA was up 5% to $1.34 billion. For the full year, it nearly hit $5 billion, up 20%, while investing $3.3 billion in growth cap-ex. Even with its large growth spending, it generated $539 million in free cash flow, an improvement from 2024, though we will likely see a decline here in 2026 given even greater capital spending.

Its Permian volumes increased by 11% in 2025. Thanks to positioning in prime acreage and an integrated “wellhead to water strategy,” TRGP has been able to grow even faster than basin-wide production. Given the strength in production out of this basin, it is planning a new Delaware Basin processing plant while making initial plans for two more that could be opened in 2028. Once these projects are complete, we should see capex come down. Meanwhile, they will begin producing revenue, adding incremental operating cash flow. Combined, this positions Targa for meaningful inflection in free cash flow over the next three years.

This growth is unlikely to stop. Looking forward, Targa is guiding to $5.4-$5.6 billion of EBITDA in 2026, up 11% from last year. It will spend $4.5 billion on growth projects, up $1.2 billion. 2026 should represent a peak year for spending.

Targa Has A Compelling CapEx Plan

CapEx is so high as it works to build the Speedway NGL pipeline system out. This will directly connect its Permian operations to Mont Belvieu. It will have an initial capacity of 500,000 barrels/day with the scope of doubling. There is scope to double the capacity, and it should begin producing revenue by Q4 2027. For those unaware, Mont Belvieu is the key site of NGL, setting the benchmark for LPG pricing. Additionally, from here, product can travel to export terminals along the Gulf Coast for sale in the global market.

This is essential because selling to the export market is a critical underpinning of production growth in the Permian. Targa is undertaking a “wellhead to water strategy” that can carry product from the Permian right to export, a key reason it has seen stronger volume growth. Obviously, the war with Iran has caused significant disruption in global markets. Given the fact that US infrastructure is being fully utilized, I see limited benefits to Targa today, especially as Speedway is not yet operational. Over the medium term, though, this crisis, I believe, can boost the relative attractiveness of US exports as Asian buyers in particular seek to ensure predictable supply. While there is no impact on near-term fundamentals for TRGP, the events of the past two months likely boost the duration of the growth story and increase my conviction in my core view that US energy exports will continue to grow.

Once Speedway is complete, run-rate growth spending should be closer to $2.5 billion, assuming ~10% volume growth across its Permian system. This is higher than its prior estimate of $1.7 billion, as its discussions with producers point to greater production growth. These bullish conversations were occurring even before the Iran war.

Shares Remain Attractive Before Earnings

Now, Targa is slated to report Q1 results on May 7th, and analyst optimism has been building with 7 estimate increases and no cuts over the past ninety days. I expect to see solid results with continued growth in Permian flows. If anything, margins may be a bit better given March commodity volatility. However, I believe the outlook will be even more crucial. I do not expect a change in 2026 guidance; however, I expect TRGP to speak even more optimistically about medium-term growth prospects given greater appetite for US exports.

Back in November, I was targeting $5.25 billion of EBITDA and $4 billion of distributable cash flow. I now expect $5.5-$5.7 billion of EBITDA and at least $4.2 billion of DCF. That is over $19.35/share. Its $5 dividend is nearly 4x covered by DCF, speaking to how TRGP is keeping its payout low to fund its large growth program. As that growth program matures, that frees up significant cash flow for dividend hikes.

I like to see pipeline companies maintain at least 1.3x coverage, so its “potential” dividend is about $15, giving shares a potential 6% yield. Of course, with its growth program and completion of underway projects, DCF and EBITDA should continue to grow. Given its prime positioning in the Permian and for LPG exports, I view a ~5% “potential dividend yield as appropriate, or $290-$300, reflecting at least 5% long-term growth.

While this is fair value, I recognize many investors in this sector prefer current income, so I am haircutting my price target by 10%, expecting shares to trade with a discount to fair value. I believe $265 is a reasonable price target. Even after this large run, I see further upside as the medium-term outlook is very robust. I remain a buy on TRGP.

data.title: I Never Knew My First Develop Deal Would Lead To A $231 Billion Marketplace
data.description:
After graduating from college and starting work in commercial real estate, I quickly learned that the real way to create wealth was not as a broker but as a landlord.

There was just one problem: I had no tenants and no money.

So, I kept working and spent several years learning the business (leasing, construction, site negotiations, etc.) before I could launch my own career as a real estate developer.

One day, around three years into my new job as a leasing agent, I picked up the phone and called Advance Auto Parts (AAP), a privately held company. At the time, the Roanoke-based auto parts chain had around 50 stores and was looking to expand south into the Carolinas.

I remember calling the company's real estate manager, a man named Gus, and telling him that I had a site in a small town called Laurens, South Carolina.

A few weeks later, Gus and I met in Laurens, and we walked the property. I discussed my proposal with him, which included the construction timeline and proposed lease terms.

Not long after that, Gus presented the deal to Advance Auto's real estate committee, and it was approved. That was my first freestanding development for Advance Auto Parts.

I completed the project successfully (on time and on budget), and from there I went on to build more than 50 stores across the Carolinas, Georgia, and Alabama.

As my business grew, I diversified by developing stores for other national tenants, including Sherwin-Williams, Dollar General, Walgreens, CVS, and O'Reilly Auto Parts.

Along the way, I became deeply knowledgeable not just in construction but in the full life cycle of net lease real estate: site selection, financing, development, leasing, and disposition.

I learned firsthand why these free-standing properties were so attractive to investors: the leases were long-term, cash flows were durable, and when structured correctly, the business model could create enormous value.

In fact, my original goal was to build a company that could one day become a real estate investment trust ('REIT').

Little did I know that I would one day become one of the most-followed REIT analysts in the world….

The Big Pivot

Life oftentimes throws you a curveball…

Because of a failed business partnership and the fallout from the Great Recession, I was forced to reset my career - from a small-town real estate developer to a Wall Street writer.

Yet what seemed like a detour turned out to be an advantage because I was able to draw on more than two decades of hands-on experience in commercial real estate and turn that knowledge into a new platform. That background as a developer has become one of the most important advantages in my career today.

And that is why, in this edition, I want to return to the sector that first launched my career: the net lease REIT sector.

This is a property sector I know intimately because I have developed, financed, sold, and lived through its various economic cycles. And if you want to understand who the winners and losers will be in the net lease REIT space, you must start with one essential concept: cost of capital.

If you get that right, you can begin to understand the real moat advantages that separate the wheat from the chaff.

The Net Lease Overview

The beauty of net lease investing is simple yet incredibly powerful: the sector is built on long-term contracts that produce reliable, predictable, durable cash flow.

Unlike hotels that rent rooms by the night or self-storage and apartments that turn units over every year, net lease properties are locked into longer-duration lease contracts that often span 10, 15, or even 20 years (plus options to renew).

And because you (as the landlord) control duration, you control risk.

In many cases, especially with true triple-net structures (where the tenant pays taxes, insurance, and maintenance), there's little to no operational burden for the landlord.

Over the last few decades, I've watched the net lease sector evolve from a niche strategy into a dominant force in real estate, with a combined enterprise value of ~$231 billion.

To put that into perspective, these U.S. net lease REITs represent around 4% of the total U.S. addressable market, which is around $5.5 trillion. Europe is an even larger net lease sandbox, with $8.5 trillion in potential opportunities.

Many corporations - big and small - are waking up to the value of their free-standing real estate. Instead of owning their buildings, they're monetizing them by selling the real estate and leasing it back, freeing up capital to reinvest in their core business.

And for net lease investors, it opens the door to scale their businesses, and through REITs, investors can own portfolios tied to some of the most unique and essential real estate in the world, including not just dollar stores, drugstores, or auto parts stores, but also casinos, data centers, car dealerships, amusement parks, bowling alleys, cold storage facilities, document storage, and even jet hangars.

Of course, to scale, a REIT must also have access to capital in the form of debt and equity, and over time, the best-capitalized REITs will be the ones that deliver the most sustainable dividends and shareholder returns.

So, in this net lease REIT overview, I decided to build a "moat model" that scored each constituent based upon the following factors:

  1. WACC (weighted average costs of capital)
  2. Balance Sheet
  3. Growth
  4. Performance
  5. Valuation

Once I finish this screening process, I will provide an overview of the two net lease REITs with the widest moats that are also trading with a margin of safety.

Weighted Average Cost of Capital

As I explain in my REITs for Dummies book, "Cost advantage is a big deal for any kind of business. When a company can manufacture its products or provide its services at a lower cost, it can pass those savings on to its customers, who are then more likely to buy more from it."

I put together the following WACC model for the following net lease REITs:

As you can see here, Curbline (CURB), Agree Realty (ADC), and Essential Properties (EPRT) score the best WACC scores, as their WACC is sub 6%.

Meanwhile, Realty Income (O), Postal Realty (PSTL), NetStreit (NTST), and W.P. Carey (WPC) are acknowledged for their above-average WACC of around 6%.

Note that Realty Income and WP Carey have meaningful operations in Europe, which gives them access to euro-denominated debt markets.

European interest rates are lower than U.S. rates, so instead of borrowing at, say, 5.5% to 6.0% (in the U.S.), they can issue debt in euros, providing a cost-of-capital advantage and making investments more accretive.

The Balance Sheet

Needless to say, debt is an extremely important ingredient, as I learned (the hard way) as a real estate developer; too much of it can be devastating.

As I scan the stock market daily, I'm always looking for companies with defensive balance sheets. I put together the following screener with a most score for each net lease REIT:

As you can see, there are six companies that have best-in-class balance sheets: Essential Properties, National Retail Properties (NNN), Agree Realty, WP Carey, VICI Properties (VICI), and Realty Income.

There are four REITs I consider to be much higher risk: Modiv (MDV), Alpine Net Lease (PINE), One Liberty (OLP), and Global Net Lease (GNL). If you are considering these REITs, I suggest closely reviewing their earnings and dividend histories.

Growth

Growth is always a driver for returns, and of course, it can be artificially engineered with high leverage and lower-quality assets (higher cap rates).

As I review the list below, it's no surprise to see Gladstone Commercial's (GOOD) consensus growth of 2026 (+26%), as growth has been terrible over the last four years (-8% in 2022, -29% in 2023, -15% in 2024, and -10% in 2025). In addition, GOOD's dividend was "too good to be true" as it cut (the dividend) by 20% in January 2023.

If you scroll up to the first chart (WACC chart), you can see that One Liberty's (OLP) WACC is 7.0%, driven by high leverage. OLP has a lackluster growth history, resulting in flat dividend growth since 2018 ($1.80/sh) and an elevated payout ratio of 91% (based on AFFO per share).

Global Net Lease (GNL) is another "train wreck" with a chronic history of dividend cuts. As you can see below, consensus growth is -16% in 2026, which also puts the company in the danger zone in terms of dividend history, with a payout ratio above 90%.

The all-stars in terms of consensus growth estimates include Essential Properties, Alpine Net Lease, and Curbline.

Performance and Valuation

I thought it would also be helpful to include a sentiment-driven indicator to this moat model, so I added this 6-year (2026 YTD) chart. Keep in mind that there's limited history for FrontView (FVR), Curbline, and Modiv.

Ironically, the three best performers are also among the lowest-quality REITs - averaging around 15% returns.

Modiv and EPR outperformed significantly in 2023, and One Liberty was the top performer in 2021 (+87%), bouncing back from the COVID lows.

In terms of valuation (the final attribute in the moat model), I find Realty Income and VICI the most attractive names, and I have concluded this article with my forecasts for these two REITs.

Why Moats Matter

VICI Properties was formed in 2017 following Caesars Entertainment's bankruptcy restructuring, which split the company into an OpCo (to operate the casinos) and a PropCo (a REIT to own the real estate). In 2018, VICI listed on the NYSE with a portfolio of 20 gaming properties.

Over the last eight years, VICI has scaled its platform significantly and is now one of the largest triple-net-lease REITs, with over $4.0 billion in revenue (in 2025) and 100% rent collection since its formation (including through COVID).

Today, the company owns 54 gaming properties, 39 other experiential properties, and 13 current tenants, with additional financing partners.

In 2022, VICI achieved investment-grade ratings and S&P 500 inclusion. Meanwhile, the company has delivered sustainable earnings per share as the portfolio has grown.

As seen below, VICI shares have become attractive due to softness in Las Vegas and concerns over rent coverage with its #1 tenant, Caesars.

However, according to Truist, May and June are showing signs of resilience based on strong group/calendar comps, which indicate "signs of inflection." I will be in Las Vegas in a few weeks, attending the ICSC conference and touring assets.

VICI shares are now trading at 12.1x vs. the normal multiple of 15.3x. The dividend yield is 6.2%, and a return to normal value (15x) could result in 20% annualized returns from now. Of course, as VICI's share price recovers, so will the WACC, as the equity multiple will enhance investment spreads, resulting in wider investment margins.

Realty Income was formed in 1969, and the very first investment was a free-standing Taco Bell. The company went public in 1994 and has since become the largest net lease REIT with a portfolio of 15,500 properties (1,761 clients in 50 states and Europe). As of Q4-25, the company-wide portfolio occupancy was 98.9%.

The company has guided for $8 billion of new investments in 2026 - the highest in its history - and in order to achieve this goal, the company will continue to source billions of dollars of investments within its geographic footprint.

Realty Income continues to expand its investment profile by investing in categories such as casinos, data centers, and industrial properties. Europe has become a growth engine, with the company now having around $8.5 trillion in exposure there (19% of ABR), favorable financing (e.g., €-denominated debt at ~4% all-in cost), and a large addressable market.

While Realty Income maintains a "fortress" balance sheet, perhaps the biggest "moat" advantage for the company is its scale, which drives deal flow through large transactions and strategic partnerships. One key benefit of scale is that these large-scale deals can be absorbed into the portfolio with minimal risk.

Realty Income has scaled with partners such as Blackstone (BX) and GIC, and more recently, the company announced a new partnership with Apollo Global Management (APO), which provides the net lease REIT with another growth engine to raise $1 billion of low-cost equity.

Apollo's Target IRR (6.875%) is expected to be meaningfully lower than Realty Income's long-term cost of public equity, which will lower Realty's cost of capital over time.

Meanwhile, shares remain in my BUY ZONE, trading at 15.1x P/AFFO vs. the normal multiple of 17.7x. The dividend yield is 5.0%, and my 12-month total return forecast is 15%.

In Closing

I never knew that my first real estate deal would lead to a $231 billion marketplace, but it set me on a path to understand a simple truth: behind every great REIT is not just real estate, but a cost advantage that compounds over time.

Since I first started my career in real estate, the net lease sector has evolved into a dominant category in which the biggest players will continue to transform their balance sheets by monetizing data centers, casinos, cold storage, jet hangars, sports stadiums, amusement parks, and a host of other free-standing projects.

The next decade will separate the true compounders from the capital-constrained (sucker-yielding REITs), and the winners will be those who can raise money cheaper, deploy it smarter, and scale faster. I'll own these "wide moat" names because that's how I sleep well at night!

Happy SWAN Investing!

Author's Note: Brad Thomas is a Wall Street writer, which means he's not always right with his predictions or recommendations. Since that also applies to his grammar, please excuse any typos you may find. Also, this article is free: written and distributed solely to assist in research and to provide a forum for second-level thinking.

data.title: GSK: 'Strong Buy' On Mo-Rez Oncology Expansion Potential
data.description:
The last time I wrote about GSK (GSK), it was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "GlaxoSmithKline's Priority Review For Cancer Drug Sets It Up For First Of A Kind FDA Approval." With respect to this article, I mentioned that the company was in the process of gearing up to potentially receive FDA approval of belantamab [now marketed as BLENREP]. It was approved in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone for the treatment of adults with relapsed/refractory multiple myeloma [MM] who have received at least two prior lines of therapy, including that of a proteasome inhibitor and an immunomodulatory agent. I had a "Strong Buy" set then, and I am going to keep this rating for the time being. There are several reasons why I don't believe a downgrade is in order at this present time.

The first reason is because the company is already in the process of looking to expand its market potential with BLENREP. It did have a problem with having the drug pulled off the market for several years but has been able to make a comeback in combination with bortezomib and dexamethasone in a narrower MM market. However, the company does boast that it can compete well because it was able to beat Johnson & Johnson's (JNJ) DARZALEX in a head-to-head study in terms of overall survival [OS]. Plus, it is the only anti-BCMA agent that can be administered across multiple healthcare settings, including community centers [up to 70% of patients are addressable in a community setting]. It is currently evaluating the use of it in the ongoing DREAMM-7 and DREAMM-10 studies to potentially expand the label of the drug to go after both 2nd-line and 1st-line MM patients, respectively. However, the downside is that the current process is that the DREAMM-10 trial is only currently recruiting patients. Plus, the DREAMM-7 study using BLENREP to treat patients with 2nd-line MM is expected to have an OS data release in 2028. It has a long way to go, with sales only coming in at $22.9 million in 2025. However, the expectation is that it could eventually possibly obtain peak sales of around $4 billion. The expansion opportunities of targeting 2nd-line and 1st-line MM patients could potentially help to achieve this goal.

The second reason is the sales growth of its Oncology portfolio, which in 2025 saw massive growth. Revenues for this segment reached $2.7 billion, which was a growth rate of 43%. Besides BLENREP, the company in this portfolio has JEMPERLI and OJJAARA. Both of these candidates achieved percentage revenue growth of 89% and 60%, respectively. Its Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation portfolio reached sales of $5.13 billion, which was a yearly growth rate of 18%. This is to potentially be helped in terms of an acquisition it made, which was the ability to buy RAPT Therapeutics for $2.2 billion to bring in ozureprubart as an anti-IgE antibody for the treatment of food allergies. I wrote about this buyout in a prior Seeking Alpha article entitled "RAPT Therapeutics: A Buy As GSK Acquisition Highlights Long-Acting IgE Candidate."

This brings me to the third reason why I believe that this stock should be kept at a "Strong Buy" rating, which is a huge push to be bolder with respect to phase 3 clinical testing. This drug candidate, known as Mo-Rez [mocertatug rezetecan], is a B7-H4 antibody drug conjugate [ADC] being developed to target patients with endometrial cancer [EC] and ovarian cancer [OC]. More about this below, but it plans several late-stage studies for this program, and if successful, it believes that it could reach blockbuster potential. With massive growth observed from its Oncology portfolio in 2025, plus the ability to make an aggressive push in several late-stage studies with Mo-Rez for EC/OC, I believe that it should retain its "Strong Buy" rating.

BEHOLD-1 Data Bodes Well For Advancement Of Mo-Rez Into Two Large Market Indications

As I mentioned above, GlaxoSmithKline is doing very well in terms of growth for its Oncology portfolio, which grew the most in the Specialty Medicines Category for it. What I'm about to go over now is the ability for the company to potentially develop another blockbuster market opportunity. This would be with respect to evaluating the use of B7-H4 candidate Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with endometrial cancer and platinum-resistant ovarian cancer. As a matter of fact, it is going to run an extensive program involving 5 global pivotal phase 3 trials in 2026. Not only that, but it was able to report positive results from its phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study using this candidate to treat both of these patient populations. Before going over the clinical data from this early-stage trial, plus any catalysts to be released from this program, I believe that it is first important to go over what these cancers are and what the possible market opportunity for the company could end up being.

The thing is that the targeting of both of these cancers makes sense because they have high recurrence rates with advanced disease. Such recurrence is observed in up to 67% of endometrial cancer patients and 70% of ovarian cancer patients. Endometrial cancer [EC] is characterized as the growth of abnormal cells and the formation of a solid tumor in the uterus of a patient. Specifically, this cancer is in terms of the cells that line the uterus and in the upper part of it. That's because cancer that forms near the cervix [bottom part of the uterus] is known as cervical cancer. Some symptoms that these patients might experience are:

  • Pain in the pelvic area
  • Abnormal vaginal bleeding
  • Change in menstrual cycle

The global endometrial cancer market size is projected to reach $40.66 billion by 2031. This is one large market opportunity that the company could potentially go after. The other one involves developing B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with ovarian cancer. First and foremost, ovarian cancer [OC], as the name suggests, is the formation of a tumor on the ovaries of a patient. The two ovaries in place are responsible for producing eggs and, in addition, for the hormones estrogen and progesterone. This cancer is devastating because it can spread to nearby tissues as well. Plus, in the early stages of disease, there are no symptoms that occur. What's even worse is that even when symptoms are present, they might mimic those of another disease. The ovarian cancer market, in the 7 major markets, is expected to reach $5.7 billion in 2034. This is a pretty good market, but it is important to note something immediately, which is that a specific group of patients is being targeted. The goal is to only go after patients with platinum-resistant disease, or platinum-resistant ovarian cancer [PROC]. PROC is defined as a patient with ovarian cancer who has a recurrence of the disease within a 6 month period after having received multiple lines of chemotherapy.

The thing with Mo-Rez is that it is an anti-B7-H4 candidate that specifically targets the immune checkpoint protein B7-H4 expressed on a variety of solid tumors. What makes this a promising target is that it is widely expressed in tumors but has limited expression in healthy tissues, making it a prime target. The main thing about B7-H4 is that it suppresses the immune system. How so? Well, there are three key reasons why:

  • T-cell exhaustion
  • Substantial increase in Regulatory T cells [Tregs]
  • Recruitment of tumor-associated macrophages [TAMs]

Another reason why the targeting of ovarian cancer and endometrial cancer makes sense is because B7-H4 is highly expressed in them. Consider that B7-H4 expression in OC and EC is said to be 94.5% and 71.5%, respectively. Mo-Rez is an anti-B7-H4 monoclonal antibody covalently linked to a topoisomerase inhibitor payload.

This push by GlaxoSmithKline is bold, but it is necessary for it to start to build further growth for its portfolio. To accomplish such a move, it is expected to initiate 5 pivotal global phase 3 trials in 2026. This is to be split into three ovarian cancer trials and then two endometrial cancer trials. The company has good standing to start these late-stage studies; it didn't just decide to start them out of nowhere. The reason for deciding to initiate this extensive late-stage program with 5 pivotal global phase 3 studies is because of the positive findings it observed in the two-part, open-label phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study evaluating the use of this B7-H4 ADC in the targeting of these patients with PROC and advanced/recurrent EC. It is important to state that this trial was split into two different parts, as I just noted:

  • Phase 1a - assessed 4 dose levels of intravenous Mo-Rez given once every 3 weeks [Q3W] until disease progression or toxicity ensued
  • Phase 1b - dose expansion cohort of study where PROC or EC patients were randomized to either two-thirds or two Mo-Rez doses, respectively

The bottom line is that this big pharma was able to achieve highly impressive confirmed objective response rates [cORR] for this clinical trial. In PROC patients, it was noted that patients who took the highest dose of Mo-Rez obtained a cORR of 62%. Then, when a high dose of this drug was given to recurrent or advanced EC patients, the cORR was 67%. Besides this, there were other highly important findings that were mentioned. The first of which is that these response rates obtained were those of broad B7-H4 expression potential. This provides the proof that the company could go after a broad set of these patients with gynecological cancers [provides proof of concept in targeting them].

Secondly, there were response rates achieved in this trial that were said to be higher than other ADCs in clinical development. Along with the fact that such cORR was achieved, was done under the guise of barely any patients needing to discontinue therapy. Mo-Rez, as part of the phase 3 BEHOLD program, is only the beginning for GlaxoSmithKline [GlaxoSmithKline Q4 and Full-Year 2025 Corporate Presentation, February 4, 2026, slide #18]. The reason why is because it has an extensive amount of trials for its Oncology pipeline of targeting patients with hematological cancers and solid tumors. In addition, the company is not only stopping with the development of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with gynecological cancers. It is also in the process of evaluating the use of the B7-H3 ADC risvutatug rezetecan [GSK'227] for the treatment of patients with small-cell lung cancer [SCLC] in the phase 3 EMBOLD study. The development of Mo-Rez is one reason for my "Strong Buy" rating, as it has massive potential to help treat the two unmet medical needs of PROC and EC with Mo-Rez. If that wasn't enough, consider that should this company be successful in some or most of its phase 3 clinical studies, it believes that peak annual market sales potential for this drug in these two indications could end up being $2.7 billion.

Financials

GlaxoSmithKline is continuing to grow its overall sales. It was noted that in 2025 it was able to generate total sales of $44.22 billion, which was a 7% increase from the year prior. The company reports on three areas that it highlights each quarter it reports revenues. These categories are:

  • Specialty Medicines
  • Vaccines
  • General Medicine

Specialty Medicines is the category that saw the largest growth of 17% for the full-year 2025. I brought this up because there was the subcategory in Specialty Medicines where it saw the largest growth, which was that of its Oncology portfolio, which grew by 43%. BLENREP is approved in 15 markets and starting off okay. However, the largest growth drivers here for this Oncology portfolio would be JEMPERLI, which grew by 89%, and then OJJAARA, by 60%. The notion here is that this portfolio is helping this company at the present time and that the addition of Mo-Rez plus the other oncology candidates being advanced above for hematology/solid tumors could further enhance this revenue percentage growth. Of course, all are in clinical trials, and there is still the risk factor, but the potential for it to expand this segment is possible. It has reaffirmed its sales outlook for 2026 with continued growth, with core EPS expected to grow between 7% and 9%. This big pharma is expected to release its Q1 2026 financial report on April 29, 2026. There is an expectation that its EPS will range between $1.19 and $1.20 and revenue will be around $7.75 billion.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in GlaxoSmithKline. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the phase 3 BEHOLD program, which is expected to have 5 pivotal phase 3 studies initiated in 2026. This program is expected to evaluate the use of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with PROC and advanced/recurrent EC. It was able to achieve a cORR of 62% in PROC patients and then 67% in advanced/recurrent EC patients. The first risk here, first and foremost, is that there is no assurance that the cORRs achieved in the phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study for these indications are going to be replicated in the several expected phase 3 studies for this program. The company has shifted to an aggressive nature in clinical development going forward, and this starts with the extensive phase 3 BEHOLD program. If the company is not able to effectively develop Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with gynecological cancers, then it will not likely be able to meet its expectation of generating peak annual sales of $2.7 billion for this drug.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of its Specialty Medicines Portfolio achieving a boost thanks to its Oncology Portfolio of products. The Oncology unit grew by 43% in full-year 2025. This was in large part thanks to the percentage growth of JEMPERLI by 89% and OJJAARA by 60%. The risk here is that there is no assurance that one or both of these products are going to allow the company to achieve the same growth in the Oncology unit in the next quarter to be reported, especially in 2026. In turn, this could end up potentially lowering the percentage growth of the Specialty Medicines Portfolio. The flip side is that the other two Portfolios are in place to potentially pick up the slack. The Respiratory, Immunology & Inflammation [RI&I] Portfolio achieved 18% growth to $5.13 billion in full-year 2025. The acquisition of RAPT Therapeutics to obtain ozureprubart for the treatment of food allergies will only eventually serve to help this segment.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of its HIV product portfolio. This is important for the company, because in full-year 2025, it was able to increase sales of this portfolio by 11% to $10.39 billion. This was thanks to Dovato and the ability to start developing long-acting injectables. The problem is that the active ingredient in many of its HIV drugs, dolutegravir, is expected to lose patent exclusivity in 2027 and 2028. The risk here is that the company could lose out on its patents and see a steep drop in sales. The hope is to counter this by using long-acting HIV injectables and the Oncology Portfolio advancement I went over above. However, the risk here is that there is no assurance that the sales driven in the Oncology Portfolio will be enough to counter the loss of the patents for dolutegravir.

Conclusion

GlaxoSmithKline is an ideal big pharma company because despite the upcoming loss of patent exclusivity for dolutegravir as part of its HIV portfolio, it is attempting to make a pivot over to many other programs. I noted already that it was able to achieve an acquisition of RAPT Therapeutics to bring in ozureprubart for the treatment of patients with food allergies. What I also went over in this article was seeing massive growth in its Oncology unit Portfolio thanks to revenue percentage growth of JEMPERLI and OJJAARA. The hope is that the long-acting HIV portfolio and the Oncology unit, plus several other measures deployed by the company, will be enough to counter the loss of patent exclusivity of its HIV franchise.

The solid growth of its Oncology Portfolio is one reason why I believe that I should maintain a "strong buy" rating on this stock. It isn't this factor only; it is also because it has several other good things going for it. For instance, just this past week, it was able to deliver promising results from its phase 1 BEHOLD-1 study using Mo-Rez to treat patients with PROC and recurrent/advanced EC. The mode of action of this anti-B7-H4 ADC was proved with the 62% and 67% cORRs observed in these patients, respectively.

What I also didn't mention is that the company, along with its partner Ionis Pharmaceuticals (IONS), was able to achieve positive results from two studies known as B-Well 1 and B-Well 2 using antisense oligonucleotide [ASO] bepirovirsen for the treatment of patients with Chronic Hepatitis B [CHB]. A regulatory review for this drug in China for the treatment of these patients was accepted for review, with other regulatory submissions likely to follow in 2026. With extensive percentage growth of 43% for full-year 2025 achieved for its Oncology Portfolio thanks to JEMPERLI and OJJAARA, plus the bold move to move over to the development of B7-H4 ADC Mo-Rez for the treatment of patients with PROC/EC, I believe that it is important to maintain a "Strong Buy" rating for now.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
